An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. The Quad State was initially on the edge of the ridge from the 17th-21st, resulting in greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Heat issues continued through the end of July with no interruption for the southernmost two rows of counties.||High temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to near 80) produced heat index values routinely in the 100 to 110 range for most of Western Kentucky from July 16th through the 21st, with isolated higher values. Some relief came for northern portions of Western Kentucky on the 17th-19th and 21st due to more widespread showers/storms and cloud cover. ||For the entirety of the mid-late month heatwave, Paducah, KY experienced 18 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point, their record longest, along with a record 49 straight days with a 70 degree dew point. Paducah reached a 105 heat index for 8 straight days from the 18th-25th, one day short of the record. The highest heat index in Western Kentucky for the third week of July was 117 at the Murray - Calloway County Airport AWOS on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||High temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s) produced heat index values routinely in the 100 to 110 range from July 22nd through the 30th in Western Kentucky, following a more off and on heat the third week of July for northwestern Kentucky. As the ridge amplified and shower activity decreased, from the 22nd-24th, localized readings of 115-120 were observed in parts of the Jackson Purchase area of Western Kentucky. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 25th-27th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-120 with localized higher readings on the 28th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||For the entirety of the mid-late month heatwave, Paducah, KY experienced 18 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point, their record longest, along with a record 49 straight days with a 70 degree dew point. Paducah reached a 105 heat index for 8 straight days from the 18th-25th and 100 for 15 straight days from the 16th-30th, both of which were one day short of the records. Murray, KY reached a heat index of 110+ for 10 consecutive days and exceeded 105 degrees every day during the 15 day heat wave. A few readings above 120 were observed including 122 at the Murray - Calloway County Airport AWOS and at the KY Mesonet station in McLean County on July 28th, which were the highest heat indices in Western Kentucky for the heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat continued from the third week of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very dry August, during which most of Western Kentucky receiving an inch of rain or less, Severe Drought (D2) conditions developed in all of Western Kentucky during September. Far southwestern portions of Western Kentucky, from Fulton to Murray, entered Severe Drought early September. Mid-month, Severe Drought expanded across the rest of Western Kentucky. Heavy rainfall from the 19th-24th resulted in significant improvement in drought conditions with Severe Drought only continuing into October for counties near the Tennessee border which generally received near normal September rainfall. Agricultural impacts from the drought occurred heading into harvest season. Burn bans were briefly instituted in many Western Kentucky counties as a result of the drought.

Showers and thunderstorms spread across eastern Kentucky as a cold front approached from the northwest on September 24th. Following a brief lull in the evening, activity redeveloped as the front approached the Ohio River late that night and then continued east across West Virginia on the 25th. Although the front exited the area by the evening of the 25th, scattered showers continued into the night due to an approaching upper trough.||Seventy-two hour rainfall measurements ranged from one and a half to nearly five and a half inches across northeast Kentucky, with highest amounts falling in Boyd and Greenup Counties on the night of the 24th into the morning of the 25th. Runoff from heavy rain led to flash flooding on several roads in Greenup County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed in several spots, including at the small tunnel of Caboose Lane and Pond Run Road in Raceland, Red Devil Lane and Diederich Boulevard in Russell, Bellefonte Alley and Ferry Street in Russell, Argillite Road and Turley Avenue in Flatwoods, and near Washington Street and Powell Lane in Flatwoods.

Thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first evidence of tornado damage was near Powersville, along the intersection of Powersville Willow and Brooksville Powersville Roads. At this location, several trees and large branches were downed. The tornado continued northeast from here, causing sporadic tree damage along its path. ||More significant damage occurred just to the northwest of downtown Brooksville, along West Miami Street. Numerous trees were downed in this area and several homes had mainly minor roof and siding damage. One home at this location did have a large portion of its roof removed and this is where the strongest winds were estimated.||The tornado was captured on security video taken from both the Bracken County Middle School and the Bracken County High School. In the video taken from the high school, the tornado is clearly visible as it moved through the field south of Cooper Lane toward West Miami Street. ||Beyond West Miami Street, the tornado moved northeast and continued to cause occasional minor tree and structural damage. The last evidence of tornadic damage was along Brooksville Chatham Road, where a barn was completely destroyed and some large sycamore trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across the eastbound lane of Kentucky Route 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a warm and humid airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires and blocked several driveways along Kentucky Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a front pushed into northern portions of the Quad State, a SW to NE oriented line of storms organized in Southern Illinois late evening and tracked through Henderson and Daviess Counties around midnight CST. Wind gusts around 55 mph were reported five miles south of Stanley in Western Daviess County, while penny size hail was measured east of Owensboro. MLCAPE was around 250J/kg while effective bulk shear was 30kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hail measured to penny size was submitted via mPING. Time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front lingered near the Ohio River during the evening hours on the 24th. MLCAPE values during the afternoon to early evening were around 1000J/kg, effective bulk shear was 30kts, and precipitable water was 1.75-2in. Isolated showers and storms midday became more widespread late afternoon into the evening. Part of a bridge washed out near Boaz, while significant flash flooding occurred in the town of Clinton as torrential rainfall totals of two inches in an hour occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a bridge on Hopewell Rd was washed out leaving a 6ft crater. Radar estimates of rainfall earlier in the afternoon peaked at around an inch in 30 minutes with only 1-1.5 inches of rain falling during the early afternoon.

Following a very dry August, during which most of Western Kentucky receiving an inch of rain or less, Severe Drought (D2) conditions developed in all of Western Kentucky during September. Far southwestern portions of Western Kentucky, from Fulton to Murray, entered Severe Drought early September. Mid-month, Severe Drought expanded across the rest of Western Kentucky. Heavy rainfall from the 19th-24th resulted in significant improvement in drought conditions with Severe Drought only continuing into October for counties near the Tennessee border which generally received near normal September rainfall. Agricultural impacts from the drought occurred heading into harvest season. Burn bans were briefly instituted in many Western Kentucky counties as a result of the drought.

The tail end of an upper trough extended southeast into the lower Ohio Valley, but the flow over west Kentucky was weak, leading to weak shear. Instability was modest with surface-based CAPE around 1000 J/kg, but that was sufficient to allow one strong storm to produce hail south of Madisonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies fell near St Charles.

A line of scattered thunderstorms moved through eastern Kentucky on the afternoon of September 22nd in response to a passing disturbance that stirred up diurnally driven convection. Storms that afternoon were capable of producing strong to damaging winds, which resulted in downed trees and power lines across parts of Greenup, Boyd, and Carter Counties. Activity quickly swept east of the Ohio River for the remainder of the afternoon and evening before dissipating altogether.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on school property fell onto the roadway and onto parked vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above normal temperatures and an extended period of dry conditions (prompting a D2 drought for Crittenden County), enabled a dust devil to form on the 11th, which attracted significant public attention as it developed next to a Crittenden County Middle School classroom. Wind shear was modest, around 20kts, leaving microscale factors as the primary drivers of dust devil formation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small dust devil, with winds estimated at 60-70mph, formed right outside Crittenden County Middle School around 1800CST with video taken of it from a classroom inside and a construction crew outside the school. No damages or injuries occurred. The video taken from the classroom was shared by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front pushed east through central Kentucky, a line of scattered thunderstorms developed. One storm just north of the Tennessee border brought a few trees down as it moved east as another storm passing near Richmond blew trees down in an isolated area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper trough approached the Lower Ohio Valley, widespread convection moved east into Kentucky. As the system moved through central Kentucky, a north to south oriented line of convection developed, resulting in scattered downed trees in four counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down on Feather Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located on the northern edge of the Quad State throughout the day on the 14th. A localized low pressure area over the Quad State helped generate scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours on the 14th. MLCAPE values were 1000-2000J/kg with DCAPE at 1000-1300J/kg in far Western Kentucky. Effective bulk shear was limited to under 30kts. Damage reports in Western Kentucky were limited to a large tree limb and blown over basketball goal with a storm moving through Fulton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was downed by thunderstorm winds near the intersection of KY-94 and KY-1212. Further south, a 46mph wind gust was measured at KY-1128 and Alexander Rd where a basketball goal with a water base was blown backwards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper trough approached the Lower Ohio Valley, widespread convection moved east into Kentucky. As the system moved through central Kentucky, a north to south oriented line of convection developed, resulting in scattered downed trees in four counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree blocking the road near Old Pollys Shoe Store. There were also trees down on Upper Brush Creek Road and at the intersection of Angel Ridge and KY 501.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fairly dry conditions were observed from late August into September, which resulted in moderate drought (D1) conditions across portions of northeast Kentucky by the beginning of the month. D1 persisted amid a prolonged period of dry weather, with a pocket of severe drought (D2) forming in Carter and Greenup Counties during the second week of the month. No changes occurred in the third week of the month.||A few rounds of rain and thunderstorms eventually brought more substantial precipitation late in the month, which resulted in one to two category improvements in northeast Kentucky. Boyd, Carter, Greenup and Lawrence Counties all improved to D0 or better by the end of the month.
